textDisplay:
The irony is:  If you compare this 'R-Rated" movie to 1980s films.  EXPEND4BLES should get a PG-13 rating.

Just by looking at this trailer, I can already tell what you WON'T see:

- Gratuitous sex/nudity (You will NOT see boobies)
- Use of illegal drugs (Closest we got was Lundgren being a 'user' in the first film).
- Racist/racial/sexual stereotypes/comments. 

So what do we have left?  A bunch of CGI explosions and CGI blood/gore.

Is that 'really' deserving of an 'R-Rating'?  

This whole 'we're listening to the fans' is basic marketing.  I'm kinda tired of this franchise.

I don't see any movies that they have done listed anywhere!  OK! So  few of the favorites may have not been able to join in the fun for any number of reasons. It's their business, not ours. If it's not to your liking then go watch Barbie and tell your troubles to Ken! Either that or shut up and do a better job on your own! We won't hold our breath waiting for you though.

textDisplay: This could be good. The Expendables has always been a mixed bag of a franchise. E2 was the peak by far. E1 was a little bland. E3's problems are well-documented. But appealing to the gore edgelords is perhaps not the best approach; the problems with E3 were more than just the lack of gore and going balls-deep with an R-rating seems like very low-hanging fruit. But this also seems to be playing to the key strength of E2 which was the perfect balance of self-referential humor and action.

textDisplay: I feel like this series should have gotten this idea right from the beginning, and even with that it never got sight of what it was supposed to be. The whole point was to bring back old action 80s/90s stars as sort of a reunion of action storytelling, but didn't get them all legitimately together until the 2nd movie, but even then they downgraded to PG-13 ratings despite the fact that all these former stars got their rise on R ratings. Now by the fourth movie you have just *ONE* 80s action star left and a bunch of young names barely known or not at all. At this point I just gave up on the original idea and maybe will catch this one on streaming... but am also expecting more of the lame CGI blood effects this series is also known for.
textOriginal: I feel like this series should have gotten this idea right from the beginning, and even with that it never got sight of what it was supposed to be. The whole point was to bring back old action 80s/90s stars as sort of a reunion of action storytelling, but didn't get them all legitimately together until the 2nd movie, but even then they downgraded to PG-13 ratings despite the fact that all these former stars got their rise on R ratings. Now by the fourth movie you have just *ONE* 80s action star left and a bunch of young names barely known or not at all. At this point I just gave up on the original idea and maybe will catch this one on streaming... but am also expecting more of the lame CGI blood effects this series is also known for.

textDisplay:
CGI has singlehandedly destroyed the action genre. Even John Wick, which started out so beautifully, has ended up a CGI laden mess.

Action films don't exist anymore. Now it's just wall to wall CGI and green screen shot digitally, so you have, in the words of Christopher Nolan, digital animation over a digital image instead of in-camera practical effects captured on 35mm film.

Die Hard could never get made today. And the fact that a 35 year old movie still looks superior to what passes for action now tells you all you need to know about the state of the genre.

textDisplay: My issue with the Expendables franchise was never the lack of R-rated violence, it's the overreliance on greenscreen and CGI that flies in the face of the 80's throwback aesthetic they're trying to evoke and severely dulls the impact of the "hard hitting action" they're trying to sell us on. I get how practical effects like squibs & pyrotechnics and actually having your actors on location makes things more difficult and time consuming to shoot, but audiences and critics alike do tend to take notice when you put the extra effort in.
textOriginal: My issue with the Expendables franchise was never the lack of R-rated violence, it's the overreliance on greenscreen and CGI that flies in the face of the 80's throwback aesthetic they're trying to evoke and severely dulls the impact of the "hard hitting action" they're trying to sell us on. I get how practical effects like squibs & pyrotechnics and actually having your actors on location makes things more difficult and time consuming to shoot, but audiences and critics alike do tend to take notice when you put the extra effort in.

textDisplay:
let's see....you added MORE new blood which was the issue with the last film. You aren't adding legacy actors to this. 50 cent? Megan Fox? Get out of here with that BS. Only Tony Jaa and Iko make sense because they've spent years proving they got what it takes. 
This series is sadly not what it once was, and you know Stallone is either going to die at the end or halfway through because he's barely in this trailer at all, and also, as far as social media goes, he hasnt been promoting it at all. 
The entire point of The Expendables was to celebrate the heroes we grew up with, when Hollywood turned their backs on them, and support a great, fun action flick with substance. This is a bad way for them to go out.
textOriginal:
